organization.organization_background: Utkrusht is a proof-of-skills marketplace that helps agile teams find talent with strong fundamental concepts within days. We help aspiring professionals improve their profile with continuous improvement in their skills, documenting their journey and helping them fulfill their potential. We primarily build, conduct, proctor and disseminate proof-of-skill assessments in Technical domains like AI, ML, Fullstack development, High scale distributed systems, DevOps etc but do help non-tech roles like Sales, HR, Marketing etc.

role_context: A QA Automation Engineer at the BASIC level on a MERN stack product is expected to write and maintain unit and integration tests for both Express backend services and React frontend components, using Jest as the test runner, React Testing Library for component tests, Supertest for HTTP integration tests against Express, and an in-process MongoDB (such as mongodb-memory-server) or per-test cleanup hooks for database-backed tests. They typically own well-defined test cases and small refactors of existing suites independently, while relying on senior engineers for framework decisions, complex auth/session integration tests, and the design of the broader automation strategy. In day-to-day work they run tests locally and in CI, investigate failures by reading Jest output and stack traces, fix common flakiness sources (shared DB state, brittle CSS-based queries, missing await, unmocked network calls), and bring frontend specs in line with React Testing Library best practices (user-facing queries, async findBy*, userEvent over fireEvent). They are expected to mock the network layer in component tests using MSW or jest.mock so the frontend never depends on a live backend during a test run, to keep test data deterministic by seeding/cleaning Mongo between tests, and to apply sound judgment in keeping each test focused on a single behaviour rather than chaining many concerns together.
questions_prompt:
Please ensure the questions you ask cover the key practical, debugging, and review areas from the competency scope for {name}, matched to {proficiency} proficiency ({yoe} years experience).

Focus the assessment on these QA-and-Automation topic areas as they apply to a MERN (MongoDB, Express, React, Node.js) codebase, and only on these areas:
- QA foundations in a MERN context:
  - Understanding the test pyramid as it applies to a MERN app and choosing the appropriate layer (unit vs integration vs E2E) for a given behaviour
  - Authoring tests with the AAA pattern, describe/it grouping, and Jest hooks (beforeAll/beforeEach/afterEach/afterAll) at the right scope
  - Reasoning about test isolation and the cost of cross-test state leakage
  - Recognising flaky tests and reasoning about whether the flakiness lives in the test or the application
- React component testing with React Testing Library:
  - Choosing user-facing queries (getByRole, getByLabelText, getByText) over brittle CSS or test-id-only queries
  - Distinguishing getBy*, queryBy*, and findBy* and using each one correctly
  - Using userEvent for interactions instead of raw fireEvent
  - Handling asynchronous component state with findBy* / waitFor instead of fixed sleeps
  - Wrapping components in required providers (Router, Theme, QueryClient) for realistic rendering
  - Understanding act() warnings at a basic level and fixing the underlying missing await
- Express integration testing with Supertest and Jest:
  - Exposing the Express app for in-process testing without binding a port
  - Asserting status codes, headers, and response body shape for both happy and documented error paths
  - Reproducing middleware behaviour (body parsing, cookies, auth) inside tests
  - Choosing between an integration test of the full route and a unit test of an extracted helper
- MongoDB-backed test data and isolation:
  - Standing up an in-process Mongo (mongodb-memory-server or equivalent) instead of hitting a shared dev DB
  - Implementing per-test cleanup so no test depends on data left by another
  - Seeding deterministic test data through model APIs or fixture helpers
  - Identifying the typical DB-pollution bugs: forgotten cleanup, shared connection, parallel tests sharing a DB name
- Mocking the network layer in frontend tests:
  - Using MSW to intercept fetch/axios at the network layer so component tests do not depend on a live backend
  - Defining global handlers for the suite and overriding per-test for error/edge cases
  - Using jest.mock on a module-level api client when MSW is not yet wired up, and understanding the trade-off
  - Asserting on what the user sees after a mocked response, not on internal call counts when avoidable
  - Recognising the smell of a test that mocks too much and asserts the mock back to itself
- Async, timers, and reliability:
  - Replacing hard-coded sleeps with condition-based waits (findBy*, waitFor) in component tests
  - Awaiting userEvent calls, returned promises, and async helpers correctly
  - Using jest.useFakeTimers when a behaviour depends on setTimeout/setInterval/debounce, and avoiding fake timers when they would harm a real-network test
  - Identifying common flakiness sources: missing await, leaked timers, shared module-level state, race between unmount and state set
- Quality, maintainability, and review:
  - Naming tests after behaviour, not implementation
  - Keeping each test focused on a single behaviour
  - Extracting small fixture builders / render helpers without over-abstracting
  - Reviewing other people's tests for the common smells (brittle queries, missing await, leaked DB state, mock-asserts-mock)
  - Updating tests when intentional behaviour changes, instead of leaving stale snapshots or skipped tests

This is a basic proficiency, so the tasks need to match one to two years of experience only. Somebody with one to two years of experience does not have extensive production experience and their concepts are usually shaky, so the complexity of the task needs to be basic only. Let the candidate be able to finish this within 15 minutes.

Prefer tasks that assess understanding of requirements and constraints, reasoning about ambiguous real-world test situations, quality of technical decisions and trade-off analysis, identifying root causes of flakiness, proposing or implementing pragmatic fixes, and reviewing or improving existing test code in a MERN codebase.

The tasks should be realistic, short, and work-item driven, such as fixing a flaky integration test that leaks Mongo state between tests, fixing a React component test that uses brittle CSS selectors and hard-coded waits for async data, mocking out the network layer in a component test that currently hits a real Express endpoint, replacing fireEvent with userEvent and async findBy* in an outdated test, or fixing a Supertest spec that fails because middleware ordering inside the test app does not match production.

Avoid trivia, syntax recall, configuration look-ups, installation/setup steps, CLI memorisation, framework-internals questions, and pure definition-based prompts. Do not ask trick questions. Ask for practical implementation, debugging, review, or decision-making in realistic scenarios.

The goal is to evaluate applied competence, practical problem-solving, implementation and review ability, sound technical judgement, and the candidate's ability to deliver realistic test fixes within the expected time constraints for their proficiency level.
